1:
"And the whole earth was of one language, and of one speech." The witnesses note the unity of mankind in one language before Babel. On "the whole earth was of one language," the grammatical voices note the single tongue. Ellicott and Gill note that "the whole earth was of one language, and of one speech" — before the dispersion at Babel, all mankind spoke one language; the whole human race (descended from Noah's family) had a common tongue and common words; the original unity of speech, before the confusion (v.7); the one language of the post-flood world.

On "one language, and of one speech," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the unity. Calvin and Gill note that "one language (one lip) and one speech (the same words)" — mankind was united in a single tongue; the unity of language was a bond of the unity of the race; the common speech bound the peoples together (a unity later broken at Babel as a judgment on their pride). What this original language was (some say Hebrew) is variously held, but the point is the unity of speech.

On the one language, the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the setting: the unity of language is set at the head of the Babel narrative; the common tongue made possible the common enterprise of the tower (vv.3-4); the one speech, which should have served the glory of God, was turned to a proud and rebellious project; the unity of mankind, abused, would be broken by the confusion of tongues. The verse displays the unity of mankind in one language before Babel.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the unity — "the whole earth was of one language, and of one speech," all mankind speaking one tongue before Babel (Ellicott, Gill); "one language, and of one speech," the unity of the race in a single tongue (Calvin, Gill); the common language the setting for the tower (vv.3-4, Henry, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the one language. The verse marks the earth: of one tongue. The verse displays the speech: one. The verse displays the unity: of the race. The verse displays the race: united. The unity of mankind in one language before Babel displays the original common speech: the whole earth was of one language and one speech — all mankind united in a single tongue, the bond of the race, before that unity was broken by the confusion at Babel. The verse displays the one language, as men journey to Shinar (v.2).
2:
"And it came to pass, as they journeyed from the east, that they found a plain in the land of Shinar; and they dwelt there." The witnesses note the journey to the plain of Shinar. On "as they journeyed from the east," the grammatical voices note the migration. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "as they journeyed from the east, that they found a plain in the land of Shinar" — mankind, migrating (from the east, or eastward, the Hebrew is variously rendered), came to the plain of Shinar (Babylonia, lower Mesopotamia); the movement of the post-flood race; the settling in the fertile plain of Shinar.

On "a plain in the land of Shinar," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the place. Calvin and Gill note that they found "a plain (a wide valley) in the land of Shinar" — the broad, fertile plain of Babylonia, well-watered and suited for settlement; the plain of Shinar, where Nimrod's kingdom was (10:10) and where the tower would be built; the place chosen for the settlement and the proud enterprise.

On "they dwelt there," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the settling: "they dwelt there" — mankind settled in the plain of Shinar; instead of spreading out to fill the earth (as God purposed, 9:1), they gathered and dwelt together in one place; the settling in Shinar set the stage for the building of the city and tower (vv.3-4), the project that sought to keep them united against the dispersion. The verse displays the journey to the plain of Shinar.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the migration — "as they journeyed from the east, that they found a plain in the land of Shinar," mankind coming to the plain of Babylonia (Keil, Ellicott); "a plain in the land of Shinar," the fertile plain where the tower would be built (10:10, Calvin, Gill); "they dwelt there," mankind settling together in Shinar (9:1, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the settled plain. The verse marks the journey: from the east. The verse displays the plain: of Shinar. The verse displays the settling: there. The verse displays the place: of Babel. The migration displays mankind gathering in the plain of Shinar. The verse displays the journey: made. The verse displays the Shinar: found. The verse displays the dwelling: there. The journey to the plain of Shinar displays the gathering of mankind: they journeyed and found a plain in Shinar and dwelt there — the post-flood race settling in the fertile plain of Babylonia, gathering in one place rather than spreading to fill the earth, the setting for the tower. The verse displays the settled plain, where they make brick (v.3).
3:
"And they said one to another, Go to, let us make brick, and burn them throughly. And they had brick for stone, and slime had they for morter." The witnesses note the making of brick for the building. On "Go to, let us make brick, and burn them throughly," the grammatical voices note the preparation. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Go to, let us make brick, and burn them throughly" — the builders prepared materials for their project; lacking stone in the alluvial plain of Shinar, they made bricks and burned (fired) them thoroughly for hardness and durability; the deliberate, organized preparation for the building of the city and tower.

On "they had brick for stone," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the materials. Clarke and Gill note that "they had brick for stone, and slime (bitumen) had they for morter" — in the plain of Babylonia (which lacks stone), they used fired brick instead of stone, and bitumen (slime, asphalt, plentiful in that region) instead of mortar; the materials of Babylonian building, confirmed by the brick-and-bitumen construction of ancient Mesopotamia; the durable materials for the great structure.

On the building materials, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the enterprise: the careful preparation of brick and bitumen shows the deliberate, ambitious nature of the project; mankind united their skill and labor in a great building enterprise; the organized human effort, soon to be turned to a proud and rebellious end (v.4); the materials of the tower of Babel. The verse displays the making of brick for the building.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the preparation — "Go to, let us make brick, and burn them throughly," the firing of bricks for the building (Keil, Gill); "they had brick for stone, and slime had they for morter," the brick-and-bitumen construction of Babylonia (Clarke, Gill); the deliberate, organized enterprise (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the prepared brick. The verse marks the brick: made. The verse displays the burning: thoroughly. The verse displays the slime: for mortar. 4:
"And they said, Go to, let us build us a city and a tower, whose top may reach unto heaven; and let us make us a name, lest we be scattered abroad upon the face of the whole earth." The witnesses note the building of the city and tower — the crux of the sin of Babel. On "let us build us a city and a tower, whose top may reach unto heaven," interpreters expound the project. The grammatical-historical and reformed note the tower: the builders purposed a city and a tower "whose top may reach unto heaven" — a vast, exceedingly high tower (the phrase "reach unto heaven" being a hyperbole for a very high structure, "for there is nothing for men impossible," not a literal reaching to heaven); some hold the tower was a temple (a ziggurat), perhaps for idolatrous worship; the great monument of human pride and power.

On the sin of Babel, interpreters note several aspects. The reformed Calvin and Gill note pride and self-exaltation: "let us make us a name" — the builders sought fame, a name, a monument to their own glory; the pride of man, exalting himself and seeking renown apart from God. The grammatical-historical and puritan Henry note defiance of God's purpose: "lest we be scattered abroad upon the face of the whole earth" — they sought to keep themselves united in one place, against God's command to fill the earth (9:1); the refusal to be scattered, the resistance to God's purpose for the dispersion of mankind, was the heart of their rebellion. Some (Jewish writers) hold the tower was for idolatry or for security (against another flood, or as a centre of empire under Nimrod, 10:10). The various aspects — pride, ambition, defiance of God's command to disperse, and possibly idolatry — make up the sin of Babel.

On "lest we be scattered abroad," the witnesses note the rebellion: the builders' express purpose was to avoid being scattered — to remain united, build a centre, and make a name; this was direct resistance to God's will that mankind spread and fill the earth; their God-defying union and pride called down the judgment (vv.5-9). The verse displays the building of the city and tower.

The witnesses agree the substance: mankind purposed to build a great city and tower, to make a name, and to avoid being scattered — a proud, God-defying enterprise (Gill, Calvin, Ellicott, Henry, Clarke). The sin bears a crux: pride and self-exaltation ("make us a name") vs defiance of God's command to fill the earth (the refusal to be scattered) vs idolatry/false worship (the tower a temple) — these aspects variously stressed; and "whose top may reach unto heaven" is a hyperbole for a very high tower, not a literal reaching to heaven. The verse displays the Babel-sin crux. The verse marks the tower: to heaven. The verse displays the debate: pride, defiance, or idolatry. The verse displays the name: sought. The verse displays the scattering: resisted. The project displays mankind's proud, God-defying enterprise. The verse displays the tower: built. The verse displays the name: made. The verse displays the rebellion: against God. The building of the city and tower displays the sin of Babel and a crux: they built a city and a tower to reach heaven, to make a name, lest they be scattered — read as pride and self-exaltation, as defiance of God's command to fill the earth (the refusal to be scattered), or as idolatry; the "top reaching heaven" a hyperbole for a very high tower. The verse displays the proud tower, which the LORD comes down to see (v.5).
5:
"And the LORD came down to see the city and the tower, which the children of men builded." The witnesses note the LORD's coming down to see the city and tower — an anthropomorphism. On "the LORD came down to see the city and the tower," the grammatical voices note the figure. Gill and Henry note that "the LORD came down to see the city and the tower" — God is represented (after the manner of men) as "coming down" to inspect the work of the builders; not that God, who is everywhere and sees all, must come down or go to see (an anthropomorphism), but the language marks God's taking notice of, and judicial investigation of, the proud enterprise; God observes the work of men.

On "came down to see," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the irony and the inspection. Calvin and Gill note the irony — the builders sought to reach heaven with their tower, yet God must "come down" to see their puny work; the contrast between their proud ambition and the smallness of their structure in God's sight; God's "coming down to see" marks his judicial inquiry before he acts (as before Sodom, 18:21); the divine inspection that precedes the judgment.

On "which the children of men builded," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the builders: "the children of men" — the sons of men (perhaps marking them as merely men, or as the godless, in contrast to the sons of God); the proud work of mortal men, which God beholds; the structure of "the children of men," soon to be confounded by God's judgment. The verse displays the LORD's coming down to see the city and tower.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figure — "the LORD came down to see the city and the tower," God represented as coming down to inspect the work (an anthropomorphism, Gill, Henry); the irony that the tower meant to reach heaven required God to "come down" to see it (Calvin, Gill); "which the children of men builded," the proud work of mortal men (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the inspecting God. The verse marks the coming-down: of the LORD. The verse displays the inspection: of the tower. The verse displays the inspected tower, with God's word (v.6).
6:
"And the LORD said, Behold, the people is one, and they have all one language; and this they begin to do: and now nothing will be restrained from them, which they have imagined to do." The witnesses note God's word on the united people and their unrestrained imagination. On "the people is one, and they have all one language," the grammatical voices note the unity. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Behold, the people is one, and they have all one language" — God observes the unity of mankind: one people, one language; their unity of race and speech gave them great power for their common enterprise; the unity that should have served God was being used for a proud, rebellious project.

On "this they begin to do: and now nothing will be restrained from them," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the unrestrained imagination. Calvin and Gill note that "this they begin to do: and now nothing will be restrained from them, which they have imagined to do" — their united strength and one mind meant that, having begun this, nothing they imagined would be beyond them; their unbridled ambition, if unchecked, would lead to ever greater pride and evil; God notes the danger of their united, unrestrained will; the bold imagination of man, capable of great evil when united.

On "which they have imagined to do," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the restraint: God's word marks the need to restrain man's united pride; the confusion of tongues (v.7) would check their unrestrained ambition by breaking their unity; God, in mercy and judgment, would scatter them, lest their united evil grow unchecked; the restraining of man's imagined evil by the dispersion. The verse displays God's word on the united people and their unrestrained imagination.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the unity — "the people is one, and they have all one language," God observing the unity of mankind, used for a proud project (Keil, Gill); "nothing will be restrained from them, which they have imagined to do," their united, unrestrained ambition capable of great evil (Calvin, Gill); the need to restrain man's pride by the dispersion (v.7, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the united people, soon confounded (v.7).
7:
"Go to, let us go down, and there confound their language, that they may not understand one another's speech." The witnesses note God's resolve to confound their language — the crux of "let us go down." On "Go to, let us go down," interpreters divide over the plural. The reformed Gill and the dominant Christian reading take "let us" as the language of the Three Persons of the Godhead — God speaking, as it were, within himself, the plural marking the Trinity (as in 1:26, "Let us make man"); the Three Persons subsisting in one Essence, deliberating and acting together in the judgment; the dominant reading sees here, with 1:26, a proof of the plurality of Persons in the one God. A second reading (the Jewish, the Targum, Aben Ezra) takes "let us" as God speaking to the angels (addressing the heavenly council); but it is objected that the angels could not share in this divine work of confounding tongues (a work of God alone). A third reading takes "let us" as the plural of majesty or deliberation (God deliberating with himself).

On "confound their language, that they may not understand one another's speech," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the confusion. Wesley and Gill note that God resolves to "confound their language" — to confuse their speech, so that they could not understand one another; the sudden diversity of tongues would break their communication, halt their work, and force their dispersion; the confusion of tongues, the means of God's judgment on their pride.

On the confusion, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the judgment: the confounding of language was God's just judgment on the proud unity of Babel; by breaking their one speech, God broke their proud enterprise and scattered them (v.8); the diversity of tongues, the origin of the many languages of mankind, sprang from this judgment. The verse displays God's resolve to confound their language.

The witnesses agree the substance: God resolved to confound the one language, so that the builders could not understand one another, and their proud work was halted (Gill, Calvin, Wesley). The "let us go down" bears a crux: the language of the Three Persons of the Godhead, the Trinity (with 1:26, the dominant Christian reading — Gill) vs God speaking to the angels (the Jewish/Targum reading, objected to since the work is God's alone) vs the plural of majesty/deliberation. The verse displays the "let us" crux. The verse marks the resolve: let us go down. The verse displays the debate: Trinity, angels, or majesty. The verse displays the confusion: of tongues. The verse displays the confounded language, the people scattered (v.8).
8:
"So the LORD scattered them abroad from thence upon the face of all the earth: and they left off to build the city." The witnesses note the scattering of mankind and the halting of the city. On "the LORD scattered them abroad from thence upon the face of all the earth," the grammatical voices note the dispersion. Ellicott and Gill note that "the LORD scattered them abroad from thence upon the face of all the earth" — by the confusion of tongues, God scattered mankind from Babel over the whole earth; the very dispersion they sought to avoid (v.4) was now brought upon them by God's judgment; the peoples, no longer able to understand one another, separated and spread over the earth; the origin of the dispersion of the nations.

On "scattered them abroad," the reformed Calvin notes the irony. Calvin and Gill note the irony — the builders sought to make a name and avoid being scattered (v.4), yet God scattered them and brought their pride to nothing; the very thing they feared came upon them by their own sin; God's purpose to fill the earth (9:1) was accomplished, against their will, by the judgment; the providence of God overruling man's pride.

On "they left off to build the city," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the halting: "they left off to build the city" — the confusion of tongues halted the work; unable to understand one another, the builders abandoned the city and tower unfinished; the proud enterprise was broken off; the monument of human pride left incomplete; the judgment effectively stopped the rebellion. The verse displays the scattering of mankind and the halting of the city.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dispersion — "the LORD scattered them abroad from thence upon the face of all the earth," God scattering mankind over the earth by the confusion (Ellicott, Gill); the irony that the scattering they feared came by their sin (v.4, Calvin, Gill); "they left off to build the city," the work halted, the city left unfinished (grammatical-historical). 9:
"Therefore is the name of it called Babel; because the LORD did there confound the language of all the earth: and from thence did the LORD scatter them abroad upon the face of all the earth." The witnesses note the naming of Babel and the confusion of tongues. On "the name of it called Babel," the grammatical voices note the etymology. Ellicott and Gill note that "the name of it called Babel" — the city was called Babel ("confusion"); the name (from the Hebrew balal, "to confound") commemorates the confusion of tongues there; "Babel" (which the builders perhaps intended as "gate of God," Bab-el) became, by God's judgment, "confusion"; the name records the divine judgment.

On "because the LORD did there confound the language of all the earth," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the confusion. Calvin and Gill note that the name was given "because the LORD did there confound the language of all the earth" — at Babel, God confounded the one language into many; the origin of the diversity of human tongues; the confusion of languages, the means and memorial of God's judgment on the pride of Babel.

On "from thence did the LORD scatter them abroad," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the dispersion: "from thence did the LORD scatter them abroad upon the face of all the earth" — from Babel, God scattered mankind over the whole earth; the dispersion of the nations sprang from this place and event; the peoples, divided by tongue, spread to fill the earth; Babel the origin of the nations' dispersion (10:32). The verse displays the naming of Babel and the confusion of tongues.

The thin comment matches the verse. The verse closes the Babel narrative, before the line of Shem (v.10).
10:
"These are the generations of Shem: Shem was an hundred years old, and begat Arphaxad two years after the flood:" The witnesses note the heading of the generations of Shem, the line of promise. On "These are the generations of Shem," the grammatical voices note the genealogy. Gill and Henry note that "These are the generations of Shem" — the formula introduces the genealogy of Shem (vv.10-26), tracing the line of promise from Shem to Abram; after the digression of the Table of Nations (ch. 10) and Babel (vv.1-9), the narrative returns to the chosen line; the genealogy that leads to Abraham.

On "Shem was an hundred years old, and begat Arphaxad two years after the flood," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the descent. Calvin and Gill note that "Shem was an hundred years old, and begat Arphaxad two years after the flood" — Shem begat Arphaxad (the head of the line of promise, 10:24) two years after the flood; the genealogy of the chosen line begins with Shem and Arphaxad; the line through which the Messiah would come, dated to the years after the flood.

On the generations of Shem, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the line: the genealogy of Shem narrows from the nations (ch. 10) to the line of promise; from Shem through Arphaxad, Salah, Eber, and onward to Abram (v.26); the careful tracing of the chosen line; God's faithfulness in preserving the line through which the promised seed would come. The verse displays the heading of the generations of Shem.

11:
"And Shem lived after he begat Arphaxad five hundred years, and begat sons and daughters." The brief verse notes Shem's long life and his many children. The grammatical voices note the longevity. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Shem lived after he begat Arphaxad five hundred years, and begat sons and daughters" — Shem lived five hundred years after Arphaxad's birth (a total of six hundred years); among the long-lived patriarchs of the post-flood line, though not so long as the antediluvians; Shem begat many other children besides Arphaxad; the long life of the head of the chosen line.

On "five hundred years," the witnesses note the lifespan. Gill notes that Shem's six hundred years (still a great age) is part of the pattern of the post-flood line, in which the lifespans, though long, begin to shorten from the antediluvian ages (ch. 5); Shem lived long, seeing many generations of his descendants, even (by the chronology) to the days of Abraham.

On "begat sons and daughters," the grammatical-historical note the offspring: "begat sons and daughters" — Shem had many children besides Arphaxad; the line of promise is traced through Arphaxad, but the many other offspring are noted; the multiplying of Shem's line; the increase of the chosen family. The verse displays Shem's long life and his many children.

12:

27:
"Now these are the generations of Terah: Terah begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran; and Haran begat Lot." The witnesses note the generations of Terah and the family of Abram. On "these are the generations of Terah," the grammatical voices note the new section. Ellicott and Gill note that "these are the generations of Terah" — the formula introduces the history of Terah's family (and especially of Abram); the genealogy now becomes the history of the chosen family; from here the narrative follows Abram, the father of the faithful (ch. 12 onward); the turning-point from the universal history to the line of promise.

On "Terah begat Abram, Nahor, and Haran," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the three sons. Calvin and Gill note that Terah's three sons are again named — Abram, Nahor, and Haran; the household of Terah; the family from which God would call Abram; the chosen family at the head of the new section.

On "Haran begat Lot," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note Lot: "Haran begat Lot" — Lot, the son of Haran (Abram's brother), and so Abram's nephew; Lot, who would accompany Abram from Ur and Haran (v.31; 12:4-5) and figure in the later history (chs. 13-14; 19); the introduction of Lot, Abram's nephew, who would share in Abram's journeyings. The verse displays the generations of Terah and the family of Abram.

28:
"And Haran died before his father Terah in the land of his nativity, in Ur of the Chaldees." The witnesses note the death of Haran in Ur of the Chaldees. On "Haran died before his father Terah," the grammatical voices note the early death. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "Haran died before his father Terah" — Haran (Abram's brother, the father of Lot) died before his father; "before" means in his father's lifetime (Terah outliving his son) — an early death; the first recorded instance of a son dying before his father; the death of Haran, leaving Lot fatherless (whom Abram would take with him).

On "in the land of his nativity," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the place. Calvin and Gill note that Haran died "in the land of his nativity" — in the land where he (and the family) were born; the family's native land was Ur of the Chaldees; Haran died in the homeland, before the family's migration toward Canaan (v.31).

On "in Ur of the Chaldees," the grammatical-historical note the homeland: "in Ur of the Chaldees" — Ur, a city of the Chaldees (in lower Mesopotamia/Babylonia), the original home of Terah's family and of Abram; the place from which God called Abram (Acts 7:2-4); Ur, a centre of the Chaldean civilization (and, by some accounts, of idolatry, Joshua 24:2); the native city of Abram, from which the family would set out for Canaan. 29:
"And Abram and Nahor took them wives: the name of Abram's wife was Sarai; and the name of Nahor's wife, Milcah, the daughter of Haran, the father of Milcah, and the father of Iscah." The witnesses note the marriages of Abram and Nahor. On "Abram and Nahor took them wives," the grammatical voices note the marriages. Gill and Ellicott note that "Abram and Nahor took them wives" — the two surviving brothers (Haran having died, v.28) married; the families of Abram and Nahor; the marriages within the family of Terah; the setting of the household before the migration.

On "the name of Abram's wife was Sarai," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note Sarai. Clarke and Gill note that "Abram's wife was Sarai" — Sarai (later Sarah, 17:15), the wife of Abram; Sarai was Abram's half-sister (the daughter of Terah by another mother, 20:12), though some identify her with Iscah; the wife of the father of the faithful, who would be the mother of the promised seed (though now barren, v.30).

On "Nahor's wife, Milcah, the daughter of Haran... and the father of Iscah," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the family ties: Nahor married Milcah, the daughter of Haran (his deceased brother) — a marriage within the family; Haran was also "the father of Iscah" (some identify Iscah with Sarai, though this is uncertain); the close family relations of Terah's house; Milcah would be the grandmother of Rebekah (22:23), Isaac's future wife. The verse displays the marriages of Abram and Nahor.

30:
"But Sarai was barren; she had no child." The very brief verse notes the barrenness of Sarai. The grammatical voices note the affliction. Gill and Calvin note that "But Sarai was barren; she had no child" — Sarai, Abram's wife, was barren and childless; the note is set with emphasis ("but"), marking the obstacle to the promise; the wife of the father of the faithful, through whom the promised seed must come, was unable to bear children; the affliction that would make the promise of a seed (12:2; 15:4; 17:16) a trial of faith.

On "Sarai was barren," the reformed Calvin notes the trial. Calvin and Gill note that Sarai's barrenness was a heavy affliction (childlessness being a grief and reproach); and it set the stage for the great trial of Abram's faith — that God would promise a seed through a barren wife; the human impossibility against which God's promise and power would be displayed (Romans 4:19-21, Abraham believing in hope against hope).

On "she had no child," the grammatical-historical note the setting: "she had no child" — the childlessness of Sarai is noted at the head of Abram's history; the promise of a numerous seed (12:2) would be given to a man whose wife was barren; the long delay and the miraculous birth of Isaac (ch. 21) would magnify God's power and faithfulness; the barrenness of Sarai is the backdrop against which the promise shines. The verse displays the barrenness of Sarai.

31:
"And Terah took Abram his son, and Lot the son of Haran his son's son, and Sarai his daughter in law, his son Abram's wife; and they went forth with them from Ur of the Chaldees, to go into the land of Canaan; and they came unto Haran, and dwelt there." The witnesses note the departure from Ur toward Canaan — the crux of the call of Abram. On "Terah took Abram his son, and Lot... and Sarai... and they went forth... from Ur of the Chaldees, to go into the land of Canaan," interpreters note the migration and its cause. The grammatical-historical and reformed note that the call of Abram was first given in Ur: though Terah is named as the leader of the migration (taking Abram, Lot, and Sarai), the deeper cause was God's call to Abram, given in Ur of the Chaldees before they came to Haran (Acts 7:2-4, "The God of glory appeared unto our father Abraham, when he was in Mesopotamia, before he dwelt in Charran... Get thee out of thy country"); Abram, called by God, led the family out of Ur toward Canaan, and Terah (as the aged head of the household) is named as taking them; the departure from Ur was in obedience to the divine call. A second reading takes the call of 12:1 as given at Haran, the move from Ur being Terah's own migration; but the dominant view (with Acts 7) places the first call in Ur, with a renewed call at Haran (12:1).

On "they came unto Haran, and dwelt there," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the halt. Clarke and Gill note that the family, setting out for Canaan, "came unto Haran (Charran), and dwelt there" — they stopped at Haran (a city of Mesopotamia, on the way toward Canaan) and settled for a time; whether because of Terah's age, or for other reasons, the migration paused at Haran until Terah's death (v.32), after which Abram went on to Canaan (12:4-5); the halt at Haran, a delay on the way to the promised land. The verse displays the departure from Ur toward Canaan.

32:
"And the days of Terah were two hundred and five years: and Terah died in Haran." The witnesses note the death of Terah in Haran. On "the days of Terah were two hundred and five years," the grammatical voices note the lifespan. Ellicott and Gill note that "the days of Terah were two hundred and five years" — Terah's full life, two hundred and five years; longer than the immediately preceding generations (Nahor's 148, v.25), but in the shorter post-flood pattern; the death of Terah marks the end of the genealogy of Shem (vv.10-32) and the close of the family's stay at Haran.

On "Terah died in Haran," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the death at the halt. Calvin and Gill note that "Terah died in Haran" — Terah died at Haran, where the family had settled (v.31); he did not reach Canaan, but died on the way, at the halting-place; after Terah's death, Abram went on from Haran into Canaan (12:4-5); the death of the aged father at Haran preceded Abram's onward pilgrimage.

On the death of Terah, the grammatical-historical note the transition: the death of Terah at Haran closes the genealogy and the prelude; with Terah dead, Abram, now the head of the family and called by God (12:1), would go forth from Haran into Canaan, to receive the promises; the death of Terah is the threshold between the universal history (chs. 1-11) and the history of the patriarchs (chs. 12 onward); the close of the line of Shem and the dawn of the call of Abram. The verse displays the death of Terah in Haran.
